statement.markdown:
Given two strings `s` and `t`, determine if they are isomorphic. Two strings are isomorphic if the characters in `s` can be replaced to get `t`, and this replacement should be one-to-one.

In other words, for every character in `s`, there should be a unique character in `t` that it maps to, and vice versa.

constraints.description: The length of the string s.
The length of the string t.

examples.explanation: The character 'a' in string s maps to 'a' in string t, but the character 'b' in string s cannot map to 'a' in string t because 'a' is already mapped to by 'a'.
The character 'e' in string s appears twice but maps to different characters in string t ('g' and 'e').
There is no one-to-one mapping between characters of string s and string t.
There is a one-to-one mapping between characters of string s and string t: p->t, a->i, p->t, e->l, r->e.

hints.text: Check if the strings have the same length.
Initialize an empty map to store character mappings.
Loop through each character in s, checking if it exists in t.
If a character exists in t, update the character mapping and continue to the next character in s.
If a character does not exist in t, return false as the strings are not isomorphic.

editorial.title: Unlocking the Optimal Approach to Isomorphic Strings
editorial.intuition: The key observation is that two strings are isomorphic if and only if there exists a bijective mapping between their characters. This means each character in one string maps to exactly one character in the other string, and vice versa.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve generating all possible permutations of the characters in one string and checking if any of these permutations match the other string. However, this approach is highly inefficient due to its factorial time complexity, O(n!), which makes it impractical for strings of even moderate lengths.
editorial.betterSolution: The optimal approach involves using two hash maps (dictionaries in Python) to keep track of the mappings from characters in string s to characters in string t and vice versa.
editorial.optimalSolution: By iterating through both strings simultaneously and updating the two hash maps, we can efficiently verify if a bijective mapping exists. This approach is known to have a time complexity of O(n) and a space complexity of O(n), where n is the length of the strings.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of this approach can be proven by observing that it checks for both the existence of a mapping from s to t and the inverse mapping from t to s, ensuring the mapping is bijective. If at any point it finds a character with a conflicting mapping, it immediately returns False. If it iterates through all characters without finding conflicts, it returns True, indicating the strings are isomorphic.
editorial.dryRun: Testing with sample inputs, such as s = 'egg' and t = 'add', helps validate the approach. The function should return True for isomorphic strings and False otherwise, e.g., s = 'foo' and t = 'bar'.
commonMistakes: Not checking if the lengths of the two strings are equal before attempting to map characters.
Using only one hash map to track the mapping, which would not ensure the mapping is bijective.
Not immediately returning False when a character with a conflicting mapping is encountered.
Assuming the existence of a mapping without verifying it is bijective.

functionSignature.cpp: bool isIsomorphic(const std::string& s, const std::string& t)
functionSignature.java: public static boolean isIsomorphic(String s, String t)
